- [TLE] SPFA and queue-ordered Bellman-Ford variants assume relaxations stay near linear because the graph is sparse, nonnegative, or "almost a DAG." SLF/LLL/deque order, adjacency sorting, and push-count guards are only heuristics; worst-case families can still force O(nm) relaxation work before distances stop changing.
- [TLE/RE] D'Esopo-Pape and other deque label-correcting variants assume the deque discipline is a practical substitute for a proved priority or phase order. They can be very fast on road-like or random inputs, but adversarial relabeling and repeated reinsertions can make the queue behavior worse than ordinary SPFA expectations, and missing negative-cycle counters can leave the loop nonterminating.
- [TLE] Bellman-Ford with a constant pass budget, early-stop expectation, or a small portfolio of edge orders assumes shortest dependencies settle in a few sweeps. A legal shortest path can use n-1 edges, so a full worst-case run scans m edges for up to n-1 useful phases; trimming phases changes the failure from TLE to wrong answers.
- [WA/TLE/RE] Dijkstra on inputs with negative edges, subtractive rewards, or constant-shift "reweighting" assumes the label-setting invariant survives because the negative part is rare or far from the source. A finalized vertex or early target pop can later become cheaper; if negative-cycle or affected-vertex reporting is part of the task, missing the full detection and propagation path can also turn into loops, finite answers for -INF regions, or sentinel underflow.
- [TLE/MLE] Duplicate-entry Dijkstra with `priority_queue` assumes stale labels stay rare. Because standard heap wrappers do not remove old keys, every successful relaxation can leave an obsolete entry; the heap and pop count must be budgeted against edge or state-edge relaxations, and missing the stale-distance skip can blow up toward O(nm).
- [WA/TLE/RE] 0-1 BFS with a deque assumes every edge after modeling has weight exactly 0 or 1 and that popped states are still checked against the current distance. Weight-2 edges, vertex penalties applied outside the state graph, visited-on-push, or using push-front/push-back as a generic priority queue can settle states in the wrong order or churn through stale repeats.
- [TLE/MLE/RE] Dial, bucket, and radix-style queues assume the distance spread, maximum integer edge weight, and active window are proved from the real constraints. Large weights, long paths, product-state distances, underestimated bucket counts, or cyclic bucket wrap without stale-entry filtering can turn the intended speedup into bucket scans, oversized arrays, mixed old labels, or out-of-range indices.
- [MLE/TLE] Materializing an implicit complete or dense graph assumes formula-defined edges are cheap enough to store before running ordinary shortest paths. A complete n-vertex graph has O(n^2) edges, so building adjacency for metric closures, teleport candidates, bitmask states, or all-pair transitions can spend the memory and time that the intended implicit relaxation was meant to avoid.
- [MLE/TLE] All-pairs precomputation assumes query volume justifies Floyd-Warshall, Dijkstra from many endpoints, or an `n x n` distance table. Floyd-Warshall costs O(n^3) time and O(n^2) space, and repeated SSSP multiplies the single-source bound by the number of sources; sparse queries, large n, or extra state dimensions make the preprocessing itself the bottleneck.
- [TLE] Recomputing shortest paths after each update, removed edge, blocked vertex, or query assumes the number of expensive runs is small. Worst-case batches can change the relevant source, target, or failed edge each time, so cached distances and early target stops still pay the advertised per-run SSSP cost.
- [TLE] Local dynamic shortest-path repair assumes an edge update invalidates only the previous tree fringe. Exact fully dynamic shortest paths have nontrivial superlinear update bounds even with specialized data structures, so contest code that rerelaxes only around the old path can degrade to a full SSSP or all-pairs repair on layered adversarial updates.
- [TLE/MLE] Enumerating all shortest paths, all equal parents, or every tight path through a shortest-path DAG assumes ties are sparse. Equal-weight layers and zero-weight plateaus can contain many shortest simple paths, and zero cycles can make non-simple shortest walks unbounded; output-sensitive enumeration or parent storage must be capped by a proof, not by average tie counts.
- [MLE/TLE] Storing tight edges for every source assumes the shortest-path subgraphs are small. For one source the tight-edge graph can be as large as the original edge set, and all-pairs-all-shortest-path maintenance explicitly scales with the number of edges lying on shortest paths; product graphs or many sources can make the tight-edge store dominate the original input.
- [RE] Distance sentinels, packed integer distances, and bucket indices assume the maximum reachable path length was bounded from the real constraints. If `INF`, `dist + w`, `n * max_w`, or a negative reweighting offset can exceed the chosen type or bucket range, comparisons may silently reverse or an index can escape the queue array.
